output:
# COGS-CLIP: Calibrated Ontology-Graded Soft-target CLIP for Medical Vision-Language Pretraining

## Literature Review

- Radford et al. (2021) established CLIP-style image-text contrastive pretraining at web scale, but hard in-batch negatives can punish semantically related non-pairs.
- Zhang et al. (2020) introduced ConVIRT for paired medical images and reports, improving label-efficient medical representations while inheriting hard-pair InfoNCE geometry.
- Huang et al. (2021) proposed GLoRIA with global-local image-report alignment, but not calibrated ontology-aware soft targets.
- Wang et al. (2022) proposed MedCLIP, using semantic matching from disease labels, but with coarse labeler-dependent supervision.
- Boecking et al. (2022) and Bannur et al. (2022) developed BioViL/BioViL-T, strengthening report semantics and temporal modeling without explicit reliability-gated targets.
- Wang et al. (2022) proposed MGCA for image/text alignment at multiple granularities; COGS-CLIP instead changes the supervision distribution.
- Tiu et al. (2022) showed with CheXzero that CLIP-like CXR pretraining supports zero-shot diagnosis.
- Zhang et al. (2023) KAD and Wu et al. (2023) MedKLIP inject medical knowledge/entities into VLP, but not as calibrated pairwise soft-target geometry.
- Wan et al. (2023) Med-UniC addresses cross-lingual medical VLP bias; COGS-CLIP is orthogonal.
- Khosla et al. (2020) supervised contrastive learning and Chuang et al. (2020) debiased contrastive learning motivate handling false negatives, but not via medical ontology distance.
- Label smoothing, distillation, ALBEF, and BLIP show soft targets can stabilize noisy VLP supervision.
- RadLex, UMLS, SNOMED-CT, RadGraph (Jain et al., 2021), and CheXbert (Smit et al., 2020) make ontology-graded confidence-aware supervision plausible.

## Brainstorming

- Extend MedCLIP's decoupled training idea to exploit unpaired image-only and text-only/structured-label data, but avoid treating every pseudo-pair as equally reliable.
- Replace coarse disease-label overlap with an ontology hierarchy so related findings such as opacity, consolidation, pneumonia, and atelectasis induce graded rather than binary similarity.
- Preserve clinically important attributes—laterality, anatomical location, severity, uncertainty, negation, and temporal change—instead of collapsing them into a small observation vocabulary.
- Calibrate noisy-label soft targets using tagger agreement, extraction entropy, uncertainty/negation cues, and rule-versus-neural disagreement so weak pseudo-semantics do not dominate training.
- Add a do-no-harm gate that interpolates back to standard hard-pair InfoNCE when semantic supervision is missing, contradictory, or low-confidence.
- Choose COGS-CLIP because it unifies these directions in one objective: ontology-graded when semantics are trustworthy, CLIP/ConVIRT-like when they are not, and MedCLIP-like as a flat high-confidence special case.

## Summary of Collective Ideas

The team converged on COGS-CLIP: a calibrated ontology-graded soft-target contrastive pretraining method for medical vision-language learning. The core insight is that MedCLIP's semantic matching fixes hard false negatives only partially because its semantic matrix is built from coarse, noisy, overconfident labels. COGS-CLIP instead constructs a target distribution from medical concept and attribute similarity, attaches calibrated confidence to each pseudo-semantic relation, and uses a reliability gate to fall back to hard-pair InfoNCE when the semantic layer is untrustworthy. This makes fine-grained medical structure part of the training objective while preserving an empirically measured fallback path to known contrastive baselines.

## Boundary-Case Unification (provable-by-construction)

COGS-CLIP uses consistent notation across the objective: q_ij is the hard-pair target, o_ij is the ontology-graded semantic target, c_ij is calibrated confidence, g_i is the reliability gate, and t_ij is the final training target. With confidence=1 and a flat ontology where all coarse shared-label off-pairs are equally similar, o_ij becomes MedCLIP's binary semantic matching matrix and COGS-CLIP recovers MedCLIP. With gate closed or confidence=0, t_ij=q_ij and the objective reduces to standard CLIP/ConVIRT hard-pair InfoNCE. With perfect labels and a true ontology-distance function, t_ij becomes an oracle graded semantic target. Ablations such as flat ontology, confidence=1, gate off, and hard-pair-only training therefore test the method's boundary cases by construction.

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can calibrated ontology-graded soft targets improve medical image-text contrastive pretraining by preserving fine-grained radiological meaning while falling back to hard-pair InfoNCE when semantic pseudo-labels are uncertain or wrong?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Medical vision-language pretraining is becoming a foundation layer for radiology retrieval, zero-shot diagnosis, report drafting, triage, and label-efficient adaptation to rare findings. Its usefulness depends not only on whether an image and a report are paired, but on whether the learned geometry preserves clinically meaningful distinctions: left versus right, upper versus lower lobe, mild versus severe, improving versus worsening, certain versus equivocal. COGS-CLIP is interesting because it turns that clinical structure into the training target rather than treating it as downstream metadata. If successful, the method would move medical VLP beyond the current trade-off between hard-pair CLIP objectives, which create false negatives, and coarse disease-label objectives, which collapse many radiologically different states into the same bucket. A calibrated ontology-graded target could make retrieval more clinically precise, improve report-drafting support by aligning images with the right finding attributes, and reduce annotation burden by extracting more value from imperfect reports. The do-no-harm gate is also important for deployment: it makes uncertainty in pseudo-labels explicit and lets the model fall back toward standard InfoNCE when semantic supervision is unreliable. Its value would be demonstrated by measured non-inferiority to the stronger baseline within a pre-specified epsilon, turning ontology use from a brittle knowledge-engineering assumption into a testable, auditable pretraining mechanism.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

COGS-CLIP is hard because the supervision we want is not directly observed: it must be constructed. First, a faithful ontology-graded target requires mapping noisy free-text reports into medical concepts and attributes. RadGraph, CheXbert, neural taggers, and rule systems disagree on negation, uncertainty, temporal change, laterality, location, and severity; concept linking to RadLex/UMLS/SNOMED can also collapse visually distinct findings or split visually similar ones. Even after linking, the distance function is a modeling choice: path distance, Resnik/Lin information-content similarity, and hand-weighted attribute penalties may rank semantic similarity differently from visual similarity in a chest X-ray. Second, calibration is difficult because there is no gold confidence label for most image-text pairs. Confidence must be inferred from weak signals such as inter-tagger agreement, extraction entropy, negation cues, and rule-versus-neural disagreement. If miscalibrated, it can amplify exactly the report noise it was designed to suppress, especially under hospital/report-style shift. Third, the components are coupled. A conservative gate can erase true semantic neighbors and return to false-negative hard InfoNCE; an over-open gate can trust noisy positives and reproduce MedCLIP-style false positives. Stabilizing this joint target during contrastive training is non-trivial. Decoupled image-text pairing also expands the target to an N x M matrix, making efficient construction and batching important. Finally, standard five-class zero-shot accuracy is too blunt to measure laterality, severity, or location gains. Naive label smoothing is class-agnostic, MedCLIP's soft matrix is flat and uncalibrated, and hard InfoNCE ignores clinically meaningful false negatives.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The closest prior work solves adjacent pieces but not the full problem. ConVIRT, CheXzero, and CLIP-style medical pretraining use paired reports as natural supervision, but their hard-pair InfoNCE losses still treat many clinically similar studies as negatives. GLoRIA and MGCA add finer image-text or region-text alignment, yet the target remains anchored in observed pairs rather than a calibrated distribution over semantically related examples. MedCLIP directly addresses false negatives with a semantic matching matrix, but its signal is limited by coarse disease labels and rule/neural report labelers: two studies that share "pneumonia" can differ in laterality, location, severity, uncertainty, or temporal change, while two reports with different labels may still be clinically close. KAD and MedKLIP bring medical knowledge into VLP, but mainly as entities, prompts, or knowledge-enhanced representations, not as a reliability-weighted ontology geometry for contrastive targets. The missing ingredient is the combination. Ontologies such as RadLex, UMLS, SNOMED-CT and structured report tools such as RadGraph make graded clinical similarity possible, while labelers such as CheXbert make large-scale extraction possible, but prior VLP systems generally assume the extracted semantics are correct enough to use uniformly. COGS-CLIP differs by making supervision both graded and calibrated: ontology distance shapes the soft target, confidence estimates down-weight dubious labels, and a do-no-harm gate interpolates back to hard-pair InfoNCE. This unifies MedCLIP-like semantic positives and CLIP/ConVIRT hard-pair training as boundary cases instead of choosing one brittle objective.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

COGS-CLIP has five methodological components. First, each report or structured label source is converted into finding-attribute tuples using RadGraph, CheXbert, and complementary neural/rule taggers. These tuples are linked to RadLex/UMLS/SNOMED concepts and retain attributes such as laterality, location, severity, uncertainty, negation, and temporal change. Second, for every decoupled image-text candidate pair we compute an ontology-graded target o_ij = f(concept similarity, attribute agreement), where concept similarity may use ontology path or information-content similarity and attribute mismatches impose clinically meaningful penalties. Third, each target receives a calibrated confidence c_ij from inter-tagger agreement, extraction entropy, negation/uncertainty evidence, and rule-versus-neural disagreement, with temperature scaling or isotonic regression fitted on a held-out proxy set. Fourth, a reliability gate g_i interpolates between hard-pair InfoNCE and the calibrated ontology target, with robustness measured by whether unreliable supervision is down-weighted enough to meet the non-inferiority criterion. Fifth, image and text encoders are trained with bidirectional soft-target InfoNCE / soft cross-entropy using ViT, Swin, or ResNet-50 image encoders and ClinicalBERT, BioClinicalBERT, or PubMedBERT text encoders. Pretraining uses MIMIC-CXR (~370K images with reports) and CheXpert (~191K images with structured labels but no public reports, used through image-only or structured-label supervision), with decoupled pairing to increase semantic combinations. Evaluation covers CheXpert5x200 for zero-shot classification and image-text retrieval P@1/2/5/10, RSNA Pneumonia, SIIM-ACR Pneumothorax, NIH ChestX-ray14, COVIDx, optional MIMIC-CXR test transfer, and a RadGraph-derived fine-grained retrieval set. Metrics include zero-shot accuracy/AUC, retrieval Precision@k, fine-tuning label efficiency at 1%/10%/100%, confidence calibration ECE, and a new attribute-aware retrieval score: the RadGraph-F1 or attribute correctness of retrieved reports/images for laterality, location, and severity relative to the query. Fairness/subgroup robustness is reported as calibration (ECE) and zero-shot/retrieval performance gaps across sex, age bands, and hospital/site shift (MIMIC-CXR vs CheXpert vs external NIH/RSNA) to verify the confidence gate g_i does not amplify subgroup bias. Robustness is tested by injecting labeler noise and requiring empirical do-no-harm behavior: COGS-CLIP should remain at least MedCLIP/InfoNCE minus a small pre-specified tolerance epsilon. Baselines are CLIP, ConVIRT, GLoRIA, MedCLIP, MedKLIP, and KAD. Ablations remove each component: flat ontology, confidence=1, gate off, or hard-pair-only training. These ablations recover the boundary cases by construction and show the unification empirically. Responsible use: MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert are used under credentialed data-use agreements, and COGS-CLIP is a representation-learning proposal, not intended for clinical deployment without prospective external validation. Expected results are MedCLIP-level or better zero-shot accuracy with comparable data, larger gains on fine-grained attribute-aware retrieval, better calibrated supervision, measured non-inferiority under noisy labelers, and MedCLIP/GLoRIA-like data efficiency, ideally approaching GLoRIA-level performance with roughly one-tenth the data.
